metadata.title: Consumer-electronics parts-pairing — Apple, Samsung, Google, Microsoft, and the 'security' lockout (both sides, dated)
metadata.status: the serialization practices, Apple's 2024 used-parts policy, the Samsung leaked-contract terms, the iFixit-Samsung breakup, and the Google/Microsoft repair postures are FACT (vendor newsrooms, iFixit, 404 Media/The Verge); the 'security/anti-theft' rationale vs the 'serialization-as-repair-monopoly + surveillance' critique is presented BOTH ways with dates; the genuine biometric-security case is distinguished from the contested non-biometric parts
metadata.frame: Deepens the Parts_Pairing mechanism for phones/tablets/PCs across the four big OEMs. The real dividing line: secure pairing of BIOMETRIC sensors (Face ID/Touch ID/fingerprint) + anti-theft (parts from Activation-Lock/lost devices) is a genuine security case advocates largely concede; serialization of BATTERIES, DISPLAYS, and CAMERAS is the contested part where 'security' shades into a repair monopoly. Dated movement on both vectors: Apple's April-2024 used-genuine-parts loosening; Samsung's leaked third-party-repair contract (customer-data upload + forced disassembly) and the iFixit breakup; Google's comparatively open Pixel program; Microsoft's Surface repairability turnaround. Attaches to spec-right-to-repair (Parts_Pairing, State_R2R_Laws, iFixit, Right_to_Repair) and the East-Asia chaebol block (Samsung_Group).

the_dividing_line: The honest split inside 'parts-pairing': (a) BIOMETRIC sensors (Face ID/Touch ID/fingerprint) have a real anti-spoofing case for cryptographic pairing, and (b) ANTI-THEFT restrictions (refusing to calibrate parts harvested from Activation-Lock/Lost-Mode devices) genuinely deter stolen-device part-harvesting; advocates (iFixit/PIRG) largely CONCEDE (a) and (b). The contested zone is serialization of BATTERIES, DISPLAYS, and CAMERAS, where pairing degrades or warns on genuine/aftermarket parts without a comparable security justification — that is where 'security' shades into a repair monopoly. grade: fact (the distinction is the substance of the debate).
apple: Apple serializes many parts and shows 'Parts and Service History' warnings; its Self Service Repair (2022) was serialization-limited. The dated loosening: on 11 Apr 2024 Apple announced support for USED genuine parts — calibration moved ON-DEVICE after install (from fall 2024), beginning with iPhone 15 and newer, covering screens, batteries, and cameras; parts detected as coming from an Activation-Lock/Lost-Mode device are restricted (anti-theft). Apple also endorsed California's SB 244 (2023) after years of opposition. Both sides: a real partial concession vs critics noting biometric and older-model serialization persist. grade: fact.
samsung: Samsung (Electronics, mobile) glues Galaxy assemblies and serializes parts; iFixit had to sell pre-glued screen/battery bundles, raising independent-repair cost. In May 2024 iFixit ENDED its 2-year self-repair partnership, citing Samsung's lack of commitment (parts pricing/quantity, glued designs). Separately, a leaked 2023 third-party-repair contract (reported by 404 Media; dated to 2023 by The Verge, story May 2024) required independent shops to upload customer data DAILY to Samsung's G-SPN database (name, address, email, phone, IMEI, complaint) AND to 'immediately disassemble' any device found with aftermarket parts and notify Samsung. Both sides: Samsung frames part controls as anti-counterfeit/safety; critics call the contract surveillance + forced destruction that conflicts with R2R laws. grade: fact (contract verified by 404 Media); rationale-vs-critique presented both ways.
google_microsoft: The comparatively open end: GOOGLE's Pixel self-repair runs through an iFixit parts/tools partnership with genuine parts available to consumers (still some pairing, but broader access). MICROSOFT turned around a historically glued, low-repairability Surface line — agreeing to a 2022 shareholder-resolution repair study, then shipping self-service tools, iFixit parts, and more replaceable components (SSD, etc.). Both are cited by advocates as proof the 'security' lockout is not technically necessary. grade: fact (relative postures documented).
the_eu_layer: Structural pressure from the EU: the common-charger mandate forced USB-C across phones (incl. iPhone, from 2024), the Batteries Regulation requires user-REPLACEABLE batteries in portable devices (phasing in by 2027), and the Repair Directive (2024/1799) mandates repair obligations + spare-parts/info — collectively constraining the serialization model in the EU market. grade: fact.
the_two_sides: Both ways (neither adopted): (A) MANUFACTURER — pairing protects biometric security, deters theft (parts harvesting), and ensures calibration/quality/safety (battery thermal); software is licensed; controls fight counterfeit parts. (B) CRITICS (iFixit/PIRG/repair.org) — serialization of non-security parts (battery/display/camera) is a repair monopoly + planned obsolescence; Samsung's contract adds surveillance + forced device destruction; warnings stigmatize lawful aftermarket repair. The dated record shows BOTH the genuine security core (biometrics/anti-theft) AND real overreach (Samsung's contract; pre-2024 blanket serialization) — and real retreat (Apple's used-parts policy, the EU rules, OR/CO/WA parts-pairing bans). grade: both documented, dated.
the_honest_reading: Parts-pairing is not all one thing: the biometric-sensor and anti-theft cases are genuine and conceded even by repair advocates; the serialization of batteries, displays, and cameras is the contested core where the security rationale is weak and the repair-monopoly critique is strongest. The dated trajectory is toward constraint — Apple's April-2024 used-parts policy, the EU's USB-C + replaceable-battery + repair rules, and US state parts-pairing bans (OR/CO/WA, devices after 1 Jan 2025) — while Samsung's leaked 2023 contract (data upload + forced disassembly) and the iFixit breakup show the practice's sharpest edge persisting. Both sides retained with dates; overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: Apple Newsroom — Apple to expand repair options with support for used genuine parts (11 Apr 2024)
iFixit — We're Ending Our Samsung Collaboration (May 2024)

9to5Mac — Apple to allow used genuine parts with select iPhones
The Register — iFixit ends partnership with Samsung over DIY repair program
iFixit — Apple's U-turn: backing California's repair bill (SB 244)

edges.raw_instrument: 11 Apr 2024 used-genuine-parts policy: on-device calibration (from fall 2024) for iPhone 15+ screens/batteries/cameras; Activation-Lock parts restricted (anti-theft) — a partial loosening of serialization
Galaxy serialization + glued assemblies; iFixit forced to sell pre-glued screen/battery bundles (raised independent-repair cost)
daily G-SPN upload of customer data (name/address/email/IMEI/complaint) + 'immediately disassemble' devices with aftermarket parts + notify Samsung (404 Media, May 2024; contract dated 2023)
edges.note: OVERLAY: surveillance + forced destruction (both-sides: anti-counterfeit vs privacy)

edges.raw_instrument: 2022 self-repair partnership ENDED May 2024 (iFixit cited Samsung's lack of commitment: parts pricing/quantity, glued designs)
Pixel self-repair via iFixit (genuine parts to consumers; comparatively open) — advocates cite it as proof the lockout isn't technically necessary
Surface repairability turnaround after a 2022 shareholder-resolution repair study: self-service tools, iFixit parts, more replaceable components
the genuine security core: secure pairing of Face ID/Touch ID/fingerprint sensors + anti-theft (Activation-Lock parts) — conceded by advocates; the dispute is over NON-biometric parts
